### ****What Is This****This lesson is about choosing your core client, it involves defining the specific group of clients you’ll target with your automated cold outreach. It doesn’t limit who you can sign as a client; it just helps focus your marketing efforts.

### Why Is This Important

-****Maximizes Value and Profitability:****By focusing on a specific group that aligns with your unique skills and experiences, you can provide more value.  
-****Enhances Personalization:****Automation works best when it feels personalized. Knowing your core client allows you to tailor your messages effectively.  
-****Improves Marketing Efficiency:****Targeted efforts yield better results, saving time and resources.  
-****Helps Identify Pain Points:****Understanding your core client's challenges allows you to address them more effectively.  
-****Builds Credibility:****Using your relevant experience as proof boosts your credibility with potential clients.

###****When****At the beginning of your business, especially before launching targeted automated marketing.

###****Who****The coach needs to do this as it is a key decision based on their career, passions and any outside influences like non competes.

###****⌛ Time****30 seconds to 3 days (only if really stuck)

##****How To Do It?**** ****This is Actually Really Simple****For 19 out of 20 coaches this should be super simple and take about 10 seconds. You just fill in the industry/business type of this sentence where you have the most experience, expertise and can add the most value.

"My core client is {industry/business type] with 10-50 employees doing £1-£20 million revenue"

# Every coach has clients outside of their core client industry!

This should hopefully help you relax about the decision. You as a business coach can coach any type of business. We are simply defining a group to improve the results for the automated Marketing, you are not limited in anyway who you work with. Let me repeat that...

# You Are Not Limited In Anyway Who You Can Work With!

* * *

### Why Do You Need A Core Client

When we come to run automated cold outreach there are a few critical parts to it being effective.

1.  ****Personalised**** - We want to be able to say "**Hi First Name, I see you run a UK Engineering Company**". This helps overcome that initial disinterest from them feeling they are being spammed. ****It feels relevant to them.****2.****Pain/Problem****- No one is ever going to pay you £2,000/mo if you aren't helping improve something in their business/life . Having a core client related to your experience helps you have a better understanding of what those problems and pain points are. So the second part of the message will be something like: "Are you looking to win bigger contracts" or "Are you looking to increase profits?" (Relevant to your core client).

3.****Proof -****Once we have asked them about the pain or problem you are going to have to back it up with proof that you can help them. This is why we leverage your experience from your career or past clients. Below are examples from coaches messaging, this proof would come after identifying the prospect (e.g. I see you run a law firm) , then using a hook e.g. are you looking to increase profit), these all worked way better than KPI:****Eileen Lancaster (Career proof)****- "I ask because at Dow I used a simple system to increase Revenue from $2M → $40M in 5yrs (32% EBIT) by eliminating low volume accounts to focus on money makers."****James Baker (Career proof)****- I ask because as an MD at JCB I used a system to grow the Attachments Business from £150K/yr profit to £9M in 4yrs by keeping the team accountable.****John McCarty (Client proof)**** - I ask because Stephen Ward, MD Clerksroom, grew his law firm’s profit from £200k to £1M in 3 years & cut his working hours in half in 1 year by following my PROFIT system

* * *

### The 5 Criteria for Picking Your Core Client

The proof is absolutely critical which is why of the 5 core criteria the #1 is value.

1.  ****Most Value****- who can you provide and demonstrate the most value to given your skillset & experience. You know the pains, know what it feels like, you will empathise with them & ultimately create a better product/service/coaching that will provide more value.

2.****Pain****- is the market in pain? Do they have a desperate need for closing the gap. A nice to have is not something people are going to take their money out and buy. Most businesses always have issues they need to solve. Are they starving for the thing they are looking for?

3.****Growing****- otherwise always fighting the trend

4.****Easy To Find****- if you are marketing & you can't find your prospects then you can't get your offer in front of them. Want to be able to easily locate them in LinkedIn sales navigator search or if you have to in associations, groups etc. They must be congregated in one place.

5.****Purchasing Power****- do they have the money. This is why you want to avoid startups and charities.

You ideally want client in the 1 - 20 million revenue range and 11-50 employees but this is not set in stone and you should not limit yourself to businesses just this size.****Companies even below 1 million in revenue will pay coaches +£3,000/mo (Ask Pam how she knows).****

* * *

### How To Make The Decision

So you want to make sure your core client hits all of the criteria with the highest weight being given to #1. There are two strategies:

****Most Impressive****What is most impressive from your career and background and then pick the person who would be most impressed by it as your core client. For example if you ran your own restaurant business for 10 years but then had to shut it down, but then grew a division of a major manufacturing company from £4M to £40M in 3 years well it's pretty clear you should pick the second one and manufacturing as your core client (Example from One of our coaches).****Most Confident****If your whole family was going to die unless you massively improved the profit and performance of a business and you could choose that one business, what type of business would you choose. Would it be an airline? Would be a bakery? So if it's no to either of those then you can try and find what it is yes to or pick a bunch more businesses and keep eliminating No until you are left with the ones you have the most confidence with.

###**"I realised I had greater value especially where my focused market place was"**

* * *

### Special Considerations

Usually there are two categories of special circumstances where your core client has to be slightly adjust from the best proof you have.

1.  ****Non Competes and Mitigating circumstances****If you are not able to target that particular type of customer because of contacts, contracts and rules then you need to pick a different core client. The best thing to do here is to figure out what businesses/industries have similar pain points to the industry you have proof in and target that similar industry. For example if you can't target care homes, you could target nurseries (Also an example of a coach).

2.****Big Business!****  
Some industries are just prone to large companies e.g. banking, pharma, airlines. In this case you probably aren't going to start coaching the CEO of one of these companies so there are two options.

3.  1.  You target the MDs of divisions within these large companies as they have "ownership" over that department. One of Pam's longest standing clients was the MD of the Fishing Division of a multi billion import /exporter.
        
    2.  You find the smaller versions of the companies that your proof and experience would still be relevant to. See the Alternative Businesses For Large Scale Industries table at the end of this page for examples.
        

* * *

### Mistakes To Avoid

There are several traps unwary coaches fall into when picking a Core Client. You need to avoid these if you don’t want to waste time and effort.

****1 | Fear of Missing Out****When coaches first think about a core client, they often fear missing out. Because picking a core client/niching your marketing down to a small part of the market means you’re excluding a massive number of the businesses you could work with. But you’ve got two choices.

1.  Be a generic coach who is trying to appeal to “anyone” and "everyone". And in the process, devaluing your skills and experiences  
    2.****Laser focus your marketing towards a specific set of decision-makers who can see why your skills and experiences mean you are their best chance of overcoming a problem or situation. And are prepared to pay you handsomely because of it.**** ****2 | Chasing The Money****The second trap coaches fall into, is picking a niche they believe is going to be highly profitable when they don’t have experience in the niche or there’s no obvious link to why they should be taken seriously in that market.****Chasing a profitable niche where you have no experience will make your life far harder than it needs to be. As most prospects will think “you can’t help me as you don’t understand me”.****Instead, serve a market you understand and have proven experience in. Or one where your skills are transferable in a way that makes it obvious why you should be paid well for your guidance.****3 | Fear of Making A Decision****The third and most common trap is NOT making a decision for fear of making the wrong choice. Picking a niche is not a once in the lifetime decision. If you pick a Core Client and then realise it’s not for you, just change. And picking a Core Client type to focus on, doesn’t stop you working with anyone else if they directly ask for your help or are recommended to you. Instead of worrying between niche 1 or 2****realise that by NOT choosing and not having focus you are actually choosing option 3****of not choosing, which is the only way to make a wrong decision.

####****4 | Choosing an unfindable or non shared pain Core Client****You have to be able to get them in a list and they have to have shared pain points. For example it's virtually impossible to find a group of family run businesses and they likely don't share the same issues when they are in different industries. Another example is a group like Military veteran business owners you can probably find but once again they will not have shared pain points so your marketing will have to be world class to connect with them over the military link.****5 | Changing Too Often****Do not change your niche at the drop of the hat. If you keep changing, people will become confused and unsure as to what you do. Use this process to pick a suitable niche. When you become the best at serving clients is when you start to see the results. if you are changing every few weeks you can’t become the best. Go all out on it for at least six months.****Then, if it’s not for you, change it.**** ****6 | Choosing Clients Who Can't Pay****

While it's noble to want to help charities or startups, it's crucial to focus on clients who have the financial means to afford your services. Working with clients who can't pay can drain your resources and limit your ability to grow your business. Ensure that your Core Clients can not only benefit from your expertise but also compensate you fairly for your time and effort. Even though it feels like you can help them, your fees will put a lot of burden on their cashflow and within 3-4 months they won't be able to continue. Get yourself to 10-20k/mo first and then if you want to help them to give back you can.

## Conclusion

You could help any business. However it's much better when you come to run automation to have a targeted list.

Do not over think this! Choose the best proof from your career and the type of industry/business it is in choose that.

If there are special circumstances choose either an adjacent industry or a smaller similar type of firm.

* * *

### ****FAQs****

****What if I choose the wrong core client?****Choosing a core client isn't a lifelong commitment. It's important to make a decision based on current insights and be open to pivoting as you gain more data and experience.****How do I know if my core client is profitable?****

Validate the core client by ensuring it meets the key criteria (Who you can add value to, Pain, Growing, Easy to Find, Purchasing Power) and through initial marketing tests to gauge interest and willingness to pay. You can make virtually any market work but it takes focus and commitment.

### What Is This?

This guide explains how to structure your coaching offer effectively. It emphasizes selling outcomes rather than hours and suggests strategies to make your services more appealing and easier to manage.

### Why Is This Important?

Structuring your offer correctly will help you:

-   Give you clarity on what you are offering
-   Communicate value beyond just time spent.
-   Make it difficult for clients to calculate an hourly rate.
-   Focus on delivering measurable outcomes.
-   Manage your time efficiently by stacking sessions.

* * *

## How To Structure Your Coaching Offer

### Focus on Outcomes, Not Hours

****Avoid Hourly Rates:****Selling coaching by the hour commoditizes your time and limits your earning potential. Instead, focus on the outcomes you help clients achieve.****Package-Based Approach:**** We recommend positioning coaching for the long-term that emphasises the results clients will get, and they pay in 12, 6, 3 month packages in advance or ongoing monthly coaching. Always focused on bridging the gap talked about in . We setup the coaching structure in a way making it harder for them to compare your services based on hourly rates.

* * *

### Best Frequency for Coaching

Should you be doing it weekly, monthly, quarterly?

****Weekly sessions****can be too intense, giving clients little time to implement changes.****Monthly sessions****are too far apart, offering only 12 opportunities per year to course-correct.****2 x Per Month****strikes the perfect balance, providing ample time for action and reflection. However, during the first 4-6 weeks, weekly sessions are ideal to achieve quick wins and build momentum.

### Ideal Session Structure****2 x 90-Minute Sessions Per Month:**** This frequency makes it harder for clients to calculate an hourly rate and keeps them engaged without overwhelming them.

You do not have to do it this way, for example Ashley Maile does 1 x half day per month with a short call in between. He found that worked better for his engineering clients. So he just combined the two slots together.

* * *

### Stack Clients for Efficiency

****Blocking similar tasks together****is a time management strategy that enhances productivity. By stacking all clients on the 1st and 3rd Tue or Wed of each month, you manage your time effectively and create a predictable routine. This approach leaves other days free for marketing activities or enjoying your lifestyle; it even allows for taking two-week holidays without affecting client sessions.

If you're still in a job, you can start by stacking sessions on Saturday mornings of when your calendar allows.

While not essential, this practice is incredibly useful for maintaining a balanced and efficient schedule.

### The Approach To Working With Clients:

# "As long as you are in business we are going to be working together."

At the start of the marketing and sales process you will focus on alleviating immediate problems and the pain those problems are creating, but during the sales call and in the first sessions you will always paint the long-term picture of working together. This helps retention.

### Quick Wins for Early Engagement

****First 4-6 Weeks:****Work with clients****weekly**** to achieve some quick wins. This initial period is critical for building momentum and demonstrating the value of your coaching. It helps clients implement immediate changes and see early results. Keeps clients motivated and committed to the program.

### Ongoing Coaching

Transition into ongoing coaching with a balance of long-term plan and short term execution on a 2 x per month sessions.

Some coaches like to sell A 6-month package upfront. This timeline is long enough to achieve significant results but short enough to maintain client engagement. At the end of 6 months, the coach sells another 6-month package or switches to ongoing monthly payments. Some coaches prefer just ongoing monthly from the start.

We will cover prices and packages in more detail next:

* * *

### Summary

By structuring your coaching offer around outcomes rather than hours, you create a more compelling and valuable proposition for your clients. Using a package-based approach with a focus on achieving specific results within a set period can help you maximize your revenue and manage your time more effectively.

Start the coaching weekly for the first 4-6 weeks to gain quick wins and build momentum, then transition into 2 x 90 per month coaching with the option.

We will cover how you sell it and the specifics of the coaching framework to make it tangible later in the programme.

### What Is This?

This lesson is about adopting a proactive mindset and taking responsibility in your quest to find ideal clients. We'll explore the concept of finding the sweet spot where your business goals, market opportunities, and search skills intersect.

### Why It's Important

-   ****Realistic Expectations****: Understand the market realities to set achievable goals.
-   ****Market Awareness****: Be aware of the abundant opportunities available.
-   ****Empowered Responsibility****: Shift from a mindset of complaints to one of proactive ownership and action.

### From Planning to Reality: Embracing Market Challenges

In the first module, you focused on ****Choosing Your Ideal Client****, a process where you had complete control over your decisions and preferences. Now, in the second module, ****Finding Your Ideal Client****, you will encounter market realities for the first time. This is where theoretical planning meets practical execution.

****Don't fall at the first hurdle****. Realize that encountering obstacles is part of the journey. Your success depends on how you respond to these challenges.

****Master The Winner's Mentality****Remember, the same mentality you adopt here is what you'll need to help your clients with. It's not about how you wish it was, but about dealing with it the way it is. As a business owner, you must be proactive and adaptable, navigating through real-world conditions to achieve your goals.

### The Overlap Concept: Finding the Sweet Spot

There are 3 core elements to this concept that must be balanced for you to be successful.

1.****What You Want****: The types of clients and businesses you ideally want to work with.  
2. ****What’s Available****: The actual businesses and clients available in the marketplace & the filtering options and data.  
3. ****Your Search Skills****: Your ability to effectively find and connect with these businesses.

The sweet spot where these three areas intersect represents you taking ownership for finding your ideal clients. It’s your job to find this overlap by being proactive, responsible, and utilizing your skills effectively.

****There Are Millions of Potential Clients****If you think finding clients is too hard, remember there are hundreds of millions of businesses worldwide and millions that are the right size for your services. Are you really going to tell me you can't find 6 ideal clients among them? It’s time to develop your skills and be proactive to go find them.

![CleanShot 2024-08-20 at 08.43.47@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2024-08-20_at_08_43_47_2x_f738470e-66a3-4903-b445-5e79f7a46297.png)

### The Mindset Shift: Proactive, Responsibility, and Ownership

-****Proactive (Action Over Complaints)****: Stop focusing on what’s wrong or difficult. Instead, take actionable steps to find your ideal clients. Complaining gets you nowhere; action propels you forward.

-   ****Responsibility (Accept Responsibility)****: Understand that it’s ultimately up to you to find and connect with your ideal clients. Blaming external factors won't help; taking responsibility will.
-   ****Ownership (Market Realities)****: Embrace the fact that you are now a business owner. If we look at how many potential clients of the size that could work with you we are on LinkedIn alone we are talking about approximately:
-   -   ****Uk -**** ****700,000 Business Owners****-****USA -**** ****4-5 Million Business Owners****-****World****-****Unknown but a lot!****  
        With such a vast target audience, there's no excuse for not finding clients. Own your search process and make it work for you.

### Follow Our System

We have designed a system to help you find your ideal clients effectively. Trust the process and follow the steps outlined in this module. Your proactive efforts, combined with our proven strategies, will lead you to success.

### Conclusion

Finding your ideal clients is all about being proactive and taking ownership of the search process. By understanding the overlap between what you want, what’s available, and your search skills, you’ll maximize your chances of success. Shift your mindset to embrace proactivity, responsibility, and ownership, and follow our system to grow your coaching business faster.

### ****What Is This****This lesson is about choosing your core client, it involves defining the specific group of clients you’ll target with your automated cold outreach. It doesn’t limit who you can sign as a client; it just helps focus your marketing efforts.

### Why Is This Important

-****Maximizes Value and Profitability:****By focusing on a specific group that aligns with your unique skills and experiences, you can provide more value.  
-****Enhances Personalization:****Automation works best when it feels personalized. Knowing your core client allows you to tailor your messages effectively.  
-****Improves Marketing Efficiency:****Targeted efforts yield better results, saving time and resources.  
-****Helps Identify Pain Points:****Understanding your core client's challenges allows you to address them more effectively.  
-****Builds Credibility:****Using your relevant experience as proof boosts your credibility with potential clients.

###****When****At the beginning of your business, especially before launching targeted automated marketing.

###****Who****The coach needs to do this as it is a key decision based on their career, passions and any outside influences like non competes.

###****⌛ Time****30 seconds to 3 days (only if really stuck)

##****How To Do It?**** ****This is Actually Really Simple****For 19 out of 20 coaches this should be super simple and take about 10 seconds. You just fill in the industry/business type of this sentence where you have the most experience, expertise and can add the most value.

"My core client is {industry/business type] with 10-50 employees doing £1-£20 million revenue"

Think about the****people and businesses you can add the most value to**** because of your experience and choose them. For example, if you:

# Every coach has clients outside of their core client industry!

This should hopefully help you relax about the decision. You as a business coach can coach any type of business. We are simply defining a group to improve the results for the automated Marketing, you are not limited in anyway who you work with. Let me repeat that...

# You Are Not Limited In Anyway Who You Can Work With!

* * *

### Why Do You Need A Core Client

When we come to run automated cold outreach there are a few critical parts to it being effective.

1.  ****Personalised**** - We want to be able to say "**Hi First Name, I see you run a UK Engineering Company**". This helps overcome that initial disinterest from them feeling they are being spammed. ****It feels relevant to them.****2.****Pain/Problem****- No one is ever going to pay you £2,000/mo if you aren't helping improve something in their business/life . Having a core client related to your experience helps you have a better understanding of what those problems and pain points are. So the second part of the message will be something like: "Are you looking to win bigger contracts" or "Are you looking to increase profits?" (Relevant to your core client).

3.****Proof -****Once we have asked them about the pain or problem you are going to have to back it up with proof that you can help them. This is why we leverage your experience from your career or past clients. Below are examples from coaches messaging, this proof would come after identifying the prospect (e.g. I see you run a law firm) , then using a hook e.g. are you looking to increase profit), these all worked way better than KPI:****Eileen Lancaster (Career proof)****- "I ask because at Dow I used a simple system to increase Revenue from $2M → $40M in 5yrs (32% EBIT) by eliminating low volume accounts to focus on money makers."****James Baker (Career proof)****- I ask because as an MD at JCB I used a system to grow the Attachments Business from £150K/yr profit to £9M in 4yrs by keeping the team accountable.****John McCarty (Client proof)**** - I ask because Stephen Ward, MD Clerksroom, grew his law firm’s profit from £200k to £1M in 3 years & cut his working hours in half in 1 year by following my PROFIT system

* * *

### The 5 Criteria for Picking Your Core Client

The proof is absolutely critical which is why of the 5 core criteria the #1 is value.

1.  ****Most Value****- who can you provide and demonstrate the most value to given your skillset & experience. You know the pains, know what it feels like, you will empathise with them & ultimately create a better product/service/coaching that will provide more value.

2.****Pain****- is the market in pain? Do they have a desperate need for closing the gap. A nice to have is not something people are going to take their money out and buy. Most businesses always have issues they need to solve. Are they starving for the thing they are looking for?

3.****Growing****- otherwise always fighting the trend

4.****Easy To Find****- if you are marketing & you can't find your prospects then you can't get your offer in front of them. Want to be able to easily locate them in LinkedIn sales navigator search or if you have to in associations, groups etc. They must be congregated in one place.

5.****Purchasing Power****- do they have the money. This is why you want to avoid startups and charities.

You ideally want client in the 1 - 20 million revenue range and 11-50 employees but this is not set in stone and you should not limit yourself to businesses just this size.****Companies even below 1 million in revenue will pay coaches +£3,000/mo (Ask Pam how she knows).****

* * *

### How To Make The Decision

So you want to make sure your core client hits all of the criteria with the highest weight being given to #1. There are two strategies:

****Most Impressive****What is most impressive from your career and background and then pick the person who would be most impressed by it as your core client. For example if you ran your own restaurant business for 10 years but then had to shut it down, but then grew a division of a major manufacturing company from £4M to £40M in 3 years well it's pretty clear you should pick the second one and manufacturing as your core client (Example from One of our coaches).****Most Confident****If your whole family was going to die unless you massively improved the profit and performance of a business and you could choose that one business, what type of business would you choose. Would it be an airline? Would be a bakery? So if it's no to either of those then you can try and find what it is yes to or pick a bunch more businesses and keep eliminating No until you are left with the ones you have the most confidence with.

###**"I realised I had greater value especially where my focused market place was"**

* * *

### Special Considerations

Usually there are two categories of special circumstances where your core client has to be slightly adjust from the best proof you have.

1.  ****Non Competes and Mitigating circumstances****If you are not able to target that particular type of customer because of contacts, contracts and rules then you need to pick a different core client. The best thing to do here is to figure out what businesses/industries have similar pain points to the industry you have proof in and target that similar industry. For example if you can't target care homes, you could target nurseries (Also an example of a coach).

2.****Big Business!****  
Some industries are just prone to large companies e.g. banking, pharma, airlines. In this case you probably aren't going to start coaching the CEO of one of these companies so there are two options.

3.  1.  You target the MDs of divisions within these large companies as they have "ownership" over that department. One of Pam's longest standing clients was the MD of the Fishing Division of a multi billion import /exporter.
        
    2.  You find the smaller versions of the companies that your proof and experience would still be relevant to. See the Alternative Businesses For Large Scale Industries table at the end of this page for examples.
        

* * *

### Mistakes To Avoid

There are several traps unwary coaches fall into when picking a Core Client. You need to avoid these if you don’t want to waste time and effort.

****1 | Fear of Missing Out****When coaches first think about a core client, they often fear missing out. Because picking a core client/niching your marketing down to a small part of the market means you’re excluding a massive number of the businesses you could work with. But you’ve got two choices.

1.  Be a generic coach who is trying to appeal to “anyone” and "everyone". And in the process, devaluing your skills and experiences  
    2.****Laser focus your marketing towards a specific set of decision-makers who can see why your skills and experiences mean you are their best chance of overcoming a problem or situation. And are prepared to pay you handsomely because of it.**** ****2 | Chasing The Money****The second trap coaches fall into, is picking a niche they believe is going to be highly profitable when they don’t have experience in the niche or there’s no obvious link to why they should be taken seriously in that market.****Chasing a profitable niche where you have no experience will make your life far harder than it needs to be. As most prospects will think “you can’t help me as you don’t understand me”.****Instead, serve a market you understand and have proven experience in. Or one where your skills are transferable in a way that makes it obvious why you should be paid well for your guidance.****3 | Fear of Making A Decision****The third and most common trap is NOT making a decision for fear of making the wrong choice. Picking a niche is not a once in the lifetime decision. If you pick a Core Client and then realise it’s not for you, just change. And picking a Core Client type to focus on, doesn’t stop you working with anyone else if they directly ask for your help or are recommended to you. Instead of worrying between niche 1 or 2****realise that by NOT choosing and not having focus you are actually choosing option 3****of not choosing, which is the only way to make a wrong decision.

####****4 | Choosing an unfindable or non shared pain Core Client****You have to be able to get them in a list and they have to have shared pain points. For example it's virtually impossible to find a group of family run businesses and they likely don't share the same issues when they are in different industries. Another example is a group like Military veteran business owners you can probably find but once again they will not have shared pain points so your marketing will have to be world class to connect with them over the military link.****5 | Changing Too Often****Do not change your niche at the drop of the hat. If you keep changing, people will become confused and unsure as to what you do. Use this process to pick a suitable niche. When you become the best at serving clients is when you start to see the results. if you are changing every few weeks you can’t become the best. Go all out on it for at least six months.****Then, if it’s not for you, change it.**** ****6 | Choosing Clients Who Can't Pay****

While it's noble to want to help charities or startups, it's crucial to focus on clients who have the financial means to afford your services. Working with clients who can't pay can drain your resources and limit your ability to grow your business. Ensure that your Core Clients can not only benefit from your expertise but also compensate you fairly for your time and effort. Even though it feels like you can help them, your fees will put a lot of burden on their cashflow and within 3-4 months they won't be able to continue. Get yourself to 10-20k/mo first and then if you want to help them to give back you can.

## Conclusion

You could help any business. However it's much better when you come to run automation to have a targeted list.

Do not over think this! Choose the best proof from your career and the type of industry/business it is in choose that.

If there are special circumstances choose either an adjacent industry or a smaller similar type of firm.

* * *

### ****FAQs****

****What if I choose the wrong core client?****Choosing a core client isn't a lifelong commitment. It's important to make a decision based on current insights and be open to pivoting as you gain more data and experience.****How do I know if my core client is profitable?****

Validate the core client by ensuring it meets the key criteria (Who you can add value to, Pain, Growing, Easy to Find, Purchasing Power) and through initial marketing tests to gauge interest and willingness to pay. You can make virtually any market work but it takes focus and commitment.

### ****Two Dimensions of Improvement****The world's economy runs on people closing the gap. The 4 main categories are health, wealth, relationships and happiness. However, when it comes to being a business coach it's best to think about it in 2 dimensions of closing the gap:

1.****Business Performance:****2. - This includes metrics like profit, revenue, lead generation, system efficiencies, and team performance.  
- Clients want tangible improvements in these areas.

3.****Individual Well-Being/Personal Performance:****4. - Clients also seek to improve their own skills, mindset, and productivity & lifestyle - e.g. if you get them home for dinner with their kids (and that's important to them) that is huge value.  
- A better-performing business often depends on the personal growth of the business owner. Also sometimes it has been affecting their health.

Different coaches will gravitate towards a different focus (and if can even vary on a client by client basis). For example:****Business Focus****- Our Global Coach of the year 2020-2022 Ashley Maile is very focused on driving the business and even says he isn't a great coach but an excellent mentor. He drives the business performance and wants clients who aren't interested in work life balance at this stage.****Individual Focus****- Whereas John McCarthy of 2023 Global Coach of The Year even though he still helps clients build incredible businesses most of his transformations talk about the health of his clients, them cutting down work hours and other aspects of getting their lifestyle back.

##****There is no right or wrong. You just want to understand how you add value.****

2.****Speed of Achievement:****They believe they can achieve their goals faster with your help.

# You are selling outcomes, futures, solutions.

## That's What They Are Buying,

So That's What You Must Be Selling...

So let's talk about how you structure what you sell...

### What Is This?

This lesson will teach you how to set the right pricing strategies for your coaching packages, ensuring that you get the best value for your services while meeting the needs of your clients.

### Why Is It Important/Useful?

-   ****Optimize Revenue****: Proper pricing can maximize your revenue and profitability.
-   ****Client Commitment****: Different pricing strategies can affect client commitment and retention.
-   ****Cash Flow Management****: Upfront payments improve cash flow management by reducing financial uncertainty.
-   ****Value Perception****: Clients often associate higher prices with higher value, which can enhance your brand reputation.
-   ****Flexibility****: Offering various payment plans can accommodate different financial situations, making your services more accessible.

## How To Do It

## ****Step 1: Understand Your Value****Before setting any prices, understand the value you provide to your clients by watching . Think about:

-   The transformation and outcomes your clients can expect.
-   Your qualifications and experience.
-   Unique methodologies or tools you offer.****Nothing Is Set In Stone****Some coaches with their experience and industry have immediately started charging more than £5,000/mo. Others have combined their coaching with consulting work for the 1st 3 months and then rolled them into pure coaching after that charging £15,000/mo for those 1st 3 months.

##****Step 2: Choose Your Pricing Models****There are really two primary way to position your prices. You are selling the same thing it is just how you package it.

###****Model 1: Monthly Packages****

****Example:****Starting at £1,450/mo

1.****Starter Price****: Begin by setting your monthly rate at £1,450 (higher if you wish but we found this is a good starting point).  
2. ****Price Increases****: Plan to increase your price by £500 every 2-4 clients you bring on so £1,950/mo, £2,450/mo etc.

****Client Justification****: Understand that with this model, you'll need to justify the value delivered every month to retain clients.

### Model 2: 6-Month Packages

We think 6 months is a good length between it's not too much cash upfront but is enough time for people to see transformation. You can always try for 12 months but that's usually with clients you have worked with and built trust. If you have lots of authority which is what we show people how to build in our top programme these longer engagements can happen sooner.

****Example: Starting at £7,800 or £9,600 for 6 months****1.****Upfront Payment Benefits****:  
2. - ****Higher Cash Flow****: Get more cash upfront, improving financial stability.  
- ****Reduced Monthly Justification****: You won’t need to justify your value each month; instead, focus on the overall transformation over 6 months.  
- ****Value Perception****: Clients are likely to see a higher upfront cost as indicative of high value.

3.  ****Payment Plans****:
4.  -   ****Full Payment****: Offer the full 6-month package at £7,800 or £9,600.
    -   ****2, 3 or 4 pay options:****We have the payment plans mapped out for you in the sales training.  
        -****Monthly Payment Plan****: Equivalent to the monthly fee, providing flexibility and a consistent revenue stream.

****Which Is Better?****Honestly the most appropriate answer is...It depends.

We don't have enough data to definitely say and I suspect it would vary massively by coach, client, industry, business stage, available cash. It also depends on personal preference and who you ask:****Pam -****prefers monthly because she is very confident in the value she adds and is excellent at retaining clients. She believes just get them signed up at the start and they'll stay for years and they don't need to keep thinking about it.****Zander****- prefers upfront because he is used to scaling through ads so cash upfront is important and likes that you can just staircase down with payment plans and end up at the monthly option anyway with the 6 pay. But he has also been used to selling online programmes that have a more defined end whereas Pam keeps clients 4, 5, 6 years (her longest was +9)****Alex Hormozi**** - prefers as long as possible upfront because of what he calls "The Look Back Window". "**Customers determine whether a purchase was good based on the last purchase they made**" so if you do great the 1st month, terrible the second month they think about cancelling versus billing upfront they look at the overall improvement during that time. Watch video below (3min) for more detail...

One way to think about making the decision of monthly or upfront

### ****Step 3: Assess and Adjust****Based on performance and feedback, be ready to adjust your pricing strategy. Incremental price increases every 2-4 clients can help you stay competitive while growing your revenue.

###****Step 4: Communicate Value****Pricing is always relative to value.

-   If you offer massive value in excess of your pricing then****you are leaving money on the table****.
-   If the value you deliver is only slightly more than the price then ****you will have a hard time making sales.****The numbers we have shared in this lesson are what works well for most coaches and business owners but always taking into consideration your industry, market, experience etc. We will show you exactly how to position your price in the sales training and we have some responses further down in this lesson if people ask you early in the process.

###****Step 5: Handle Objections****We will show you how to handle price objections in the sales training but remember from the key is in defining the value you add, which we will also show you exactly how to do in the sales training.

#****Price Examples**** ****Sharing these to give you confidence that people do pay these figures for coaching.****## £4,950 + 6 x £995 = £11K for 6 months

Anne followed the sales process and sold a 6-month coaching package on a slightly different payment plan option 50% upfront front. So it was £4,950 upfront, followed by £995/mo. This approach not only ensures commitment but also provides immediate cash flow.

## £1,450/mo, £1,450/mo, £1,950/mo

James signed 3 clients in his 1st 3 weeks at the recommended price raising his prices on the 3rd client.

## 2 x £1,450/mo Clients In One Day

As John says in the video that's*****easily £50,000*****in coaching fees coming his way.

## £600, £1,200, £1,500, £2,000

Ashley shares his price increases with encouragement from Pam asking "why are you not charging £2,000?"

## What To Say If Someone Asks You The Price****BEFORE Sales Call & Demonstrated Value****If someone asks you early on in the marketing process then****you don't want to avoid the question****. Otherwise that makes them feel like you are hiding something. However, you also don't want to give a fixed amount before demonstrating value so we recommend saying this:

> "Thanks for asking, our/my Top coaching clients investment up to £50,000 a year but you can start for under £1,000/mo, it depends on your situation and what makes sense. I could do a call on (give two days e.g. Monday or Wednesday) to look at what makes sense for you. Which day works best for you?

The reason we do it like this is:

1.  You don't want to be evasive and avoid the question
2.  We want to anchor a very high price in their mind at the start so what we say next seems less
3.  We then want to alleviate concerns that they can't get started. Even if you minimum fee is £2,000/mo I'd say the £1,000 and at the end of the call if they remember what you said just say that was for once per month coaching.
4.  You want to take the lead and assume it makes sense to have the call and then just ask which day works best presumes it, they are more likely to just pick one.

****What You Say At The End of The Sales Call****>**For The BOSS Coaching that gets you to X/year revenue....(then choose either or)**>

> 1.**It's an upfront investment for the 1st 6 months of £9,600.**>**2. It's an monthly investment of £1,450 per month.**>  
> **And for that you get...(then we list out what they get)**

You will learn exactly how to do this in the training.

## Conclusion

Choosing the right pricing strategy is crucial for maximizing your revenue, enhancing client commitment, and improving cash flow. Whether you opt for monthly packages or a 6-month upfront plan, ensure that your pricing reflects the high value you offer. Track and adjust your pricing as needed, and always communicate the transformative impact your coaching can have on your clients’ businesses.

By implementing these pricing strategies, you're not just setting a price but building a sustainable and thriving coaching business.

### Going Pro Core Principles

Notice that all these “productivity” hacks are about eliminating not adding. Whenever someone tells you “do these things that you’re not doing to have more time.” Be skeptical. Best way to make more time is to stop doing shit that doesn’t matter.****Get More Focused Time****Prioritise 4 hours a day to work uninterrupted. Early morning and late night work well since other people are usually clocked out and won’t bother you.

Have a block of your day that NEVER gets interrupted.Example: I don’t talk to people/take meetings until noon.That gives me 4-7hrs uninterrupted per day. Works for me.

Treat weekdays and weekends the same. Go to bed at reasonable hour. Wake up at reasonable hour. Work if you want to, it's not illegal! We just got 52-104 more days of productivity per year.****Focus On What Matters****Outsource personal activities/chores.This gives you time BACK. Cooking. Cleaning. Picking stuff up. Errands. Etc.Human-ing. You are a high-value business coach charging $500+ an hour...ACT LIKE IT!

Audit or remove meetings ruthlessly. See what stuff you can stop attending, attend less frequently, get an email update instead, or send someone in your place.

Look at your overwhelming to do list and put names next to people who can handle the items. Not all, but most, can be delegated. Then give a deadline for them. Then set calendar reminder for you to check on what you just delegated.

Stop replying to people who only ask for shit from you. Eventually, they’ll just stop.

Don’t check email until AFTER you’ve done what you need to get done.****Environment****Get a workspace without distractions. Notifications are the enemy. Remove all, EVERYWHERE...Your phone, your desktop, external distraction, internal distractions (deal with you limiting patterns)

Make sure you get your partner onboard by helping them understand what you are trying to achieve. You don't want a spouse to make you feel guilty for working. Cheerleaders don’t ask the quarterback to leave the game when the game’s on the line.****Consumption****Cut or Cycle stimulants. Don’t let them use you.If you can’t function without them (E.g. coffee), they’ve stopped conferring a benefit.Be able to stop them and only use them when you really know you want that extra push. On off on off is typically good enough for most, or eventually you won't need them at all.

Limit alcohol consumption. It affects sleep and mood over the next few days.It’s just a net negative from a money perspective. Not saying it’s not fun. Just saying it’s not productive. And productive is the goal.

## Principles of Effective Prospect Search (FIND)

### The Chocolate Box Analogy

Imagine you have a box of assorted chocolates and sweets in front of you. Your goal is to find the perfect group of Lindt chocolates that suits your taste. At first glance, the task seems daunting because the box contains a variety of chocolates and sweets, some of which you might not even like. To make this search easier, you need to employ a structured approach. This is exactly how you should approach your prospect search in business.

![Lindor-pick-mix-Box-720x460.webp](https://cdn.disco.co/media/Lindor-pick-mix-Box-720x460_0e15c880-6303-4578-b5b1-46a4bdacd41d.webp)

Some of these chocolates would be a lot tastier than others.

Just like there are business you are better suited to market to and coach.****Your job is to filter out the ones you don't want and find the ones you do.****### Features (F)****Step One: Define Desired Features****Just like knowing which type of Lindt chocolate you prefer, you need to know the features of prospects and get rid of everything else. Job title, Location, headcount etc. It's like removing all the sweets and just leaving the chocolates to sort through.

### Interaction & Intent (I)****Step Two: Assess Intent****Intent can be thought of as the freshness of the chocolate. You want to ensure that you get the best and freshest chocolate that will taste the most delicious. As it relates to your business it represents either prospects who:

1.  have interacted with you/your business in some way already - Their engagement level
    
2.  1.  Current network and past colleagues
    2.  Business owners you already know
    3.  The interested prospects you generate from marketing campaigns
3.  Would be most likely to interact with you
    
4.  1.  prospects you can show the most proof and credibility to
    2.  prospects in the most pain
    3.  prospects who are in the places you are searching (platform, country etc.)

### Narrowing (N)****Step Three: Narrow Down Options****

Start pulling out the chocolates that meet your specific features and intent. This represents filtering your search data even further by specific industry, perhaps even head count or any other features.

* * *

### Data Quality (D)

****Step Four: Ensure Data Quality****

Finally, you need to unwrap the chocolate to make sure it is exactly what you wanted. Sometimes, chocolates can be mislabeled, and you might open one expecting it to be nut-free only to find it contains nuts. Ensuring data quality means verifying that the chocolates meet your criteria before making a final choice. In your case it is checking the lists that you generate to make sure they are your ideal client and they fit the criteria here:

### Conclusion

Effective prospect searching is like finding your favorite type of Lindt chocolate in a box of assorted sweets. By following the FIND principles—Features, Intent, Narrowing, and Data Quality—you can create highly targeted and effective searches. Start with a clear objective, use simple and complex searches as needed, understand data imperfections, and continually refine your criteria and if so manually tidy your list. By mastering these stages, you'll save time and improve the quality of your leads, ultimately growing your coaching business faster.

## This Will Make You a More Confident Coach (Before you even sign a client)

Confidence is one of the things I hear coaches talk about most, whether you're just starting out or you've been doing this for years.

And here's what I've come to believe: confidence doesn't come from doing more sessions, getting more qualifications, or waiting until you feel ready. Confidence comes from clarity. Specifically, clarity about who you are as a coach.

When you know your archetype, when you can name the kind of coach you actually are rather than the kind you think you **should**be, something shifts. You stop second-guessing your instincts. You stop apologising for your style. You start owning it. And when you own it, your clients feel it.

That's what this post is about.

## Three Traits That Define Your Coaching Style

After working with coaches across this community, I've found there are three dimensions that shape how you show up in a session. None of these is better or worse than any other. They're just different. And knowing where you sit changes everything.****1. Mentor to Coach****(1 = pure mentor, 5 = pure coach)

A mentor draws on their own experience. They've walked the path, they know what worked, and they share it. There's real power in that. Business owners often desperately need someone who's been there to just tell them what to do.

A pure coach believes the answer is already inside the client. Their job is to ask the question that helps the client hear themselves think. They're not there to give the answer. They're there to make the question unavoidable.

Ashley sits firmly at the mentor end. He's built businesses, he knows what works, and he'll tell you clearly. Mark is at the other extreme. He'll rarely share his own view in a session. He'll ask you questions until you arrive at the answer yourself, and when you do, it sticks in a way that being told never quite does. Pam sits somewhere in the middle, blending both approaches depending on what the client needs in the moment.****2. Growth-Focused to Lifestyle-Focused****(1 = growth-first, 5 = lifestyle-first)

Some coaches are wired to help clients scale. Revenue, team, market share. When they look at a business, they see opportunity and they want to chase it. That energy is infectious and it moves people.

Others believe the business exists to serve the life, not the other way around. They think holistically about the owner's health, relationships, and fulfilment, not just the P&L. They'll push back when a client is winning in business but losing in life.

Ashley is a 1 here too. He is focused on growth and he makes no apology for it. He only takes on clients who genuinely want to scale their business. When someone comes to him wanting primarily lifestyle balance, he refers them on, because he knows another coach will serve them far better. Pam sits at the other end of this scale, and there's a personal reason for that. She built a business that nearly cost her everything, her health, her relationships, the very life she was building the business for. That experience shaped her deeply. She still wants her clients to grow. But she will never let them lose sight of why they're building it. The business should serve the life. When it starts consuming the life instead, Pam will name it.****3. Tough Love to Nurturing****(1 = tough love, 5 = nurturing)

Some coaches challenge hard. They hold clients accountable with very little softening, call out the stories clients tell themselves, and create pressure that produces results. Clients who need a push thrive with them. Imagine Gordon Ramsay feedback but a little less swearing!

Others lead with warmth. They create safety, they encourage, they meet the client exactly where they are, and from that place of trust, remarkable things happen. Clients who need permission to believe in themselves thrive with them.

Ashley brings tough love. He will hold your feet to the fire and he won't dress it up. I don't know for certain but going on personalities imagine people like James Baker leads with genuine warmth, challenging clients but encouraging. He creates a space where clients feel safe enough to be truly honest, and then he uses that honesty to move them forward. Both get extraordinary results. The difference is the environment they create to get there.

### So what is your style?

Below I created a little tool for you to play with to see the different coaches and their style and you can think about how you may compare. Click on a coaches name to apply what i believe is their score on the scales.

## Why This Matters for Your Confidence

Here's what happens when you don't know your archetype: you try to be everything. You're tough when you think you should be tough and warm when you think you should be warm, and neither feels natural because you're performing a version of coaching rather than actually being a coach.

Here's what happens when you do know: you stop performing. You make decisions in the session from instinct rather than anxiety. A client pushes back and instead of wobbling, you hold your position, because you know why you do what you do.

Look at Ashley. He is completely clear on who he is and who he serves. That clarity means he never wastes energy trying to be something he's not. Look at Pam. Her experience gives her a perspective that no qualification could teach, and knowing that is part of what makes her so effective. Look at Mark. He trusts the process of pure coaching so completely that clients trust it too, even when it feels uncomfortable. That trust comes from his clarity about who he is.

Knowing your archetype also means knowing your ideal client. When you're clear on your style, you naturally attract the people who will thrive with you, and you can refer on the people who won't, with confidence rather than guilt.****If you try to appeal to everyone you appeal to no one!****## We Want to Hear From You

Drop your scores in the comments below. Give yourself a number from 1 to 5 on each of the three traits:****Mentor to Coach****— 1 means "I draw on my experience and tell people what to do." 5 means "I rarely share my own view. I draw everything out of the client."****Growth to Lifestyle****— 1 means "I'm focused on scaling the business above all else." 5 means "I think holistically about life and business together."****Tough Love to Nurturing**** — 1 means "I challenge hard and don't soften much." 5 means "I lead with warmth, encouragement, and emotional safety."

Your comment might look something like this: "I'm a 2-4-3. I lean mentor, I think holistically, and I'm somewhere in the middle on tough vs warm."

One thing I'd ask: be honest rather than aspirational. The goal isn't to post the score that sounds most impressive. It's to name what's actually true. That's where the clarity comes from. And that's where the confidence follows.

I'd love to see how we're all spread across the scale. I suspect it'll spark a really good conversation.

## ****Purpose****

## To share with you the only two elements you MUST focus on to achieve results. Everything else is irrelevant to getting clients. By mastering these concepts and following this proven formula, you're setting yourself up for guaranteed success. This is so important because if you ever struggle to sign clients, the issue and solution is in this lesson.You cannot fail, if you follow these stepsIf you apply these strategies consistently, you cannot fail. This lesson will be your go-to guide whenever you need to refocus and ensure you're on the path to achieving your business goals.Introduction: The Simple Formula for SuccessSuccess in signing clients as a business coach can be boiled down to a simple formula: 

## Volume x Conversion = Results

Understanding and optimising these two factors will lead to increased business success. In fact it is the ONLY thing that can.****If you have struggled to achieve results****it's because one or BOTH of these were missing.****Understanding Volume and Conversion****Let's break these two concepts down into their core definitions and then build a strategy from there.****Understanding Volume****Volume is about how many people you reach out to with your coaching offer. This isn't about selling right away; it's about seeing if they're interested in solving a pain/problem they have. The more people you reach out to, the better your chances of finding potential clients. Volume has two key parts:

1.****Outreach Rate****: This is how many people you contact in a given time period, like daily or weekly. It's important to be aware of the outreach limits on platforms you are using. For example LinkedIn, you can only send 100-200 connection requests per week. You can't suddenly send 5,000 requests, so your outreach should align with these capacity limits, and this is why the next concept then becomes important...

2. **Consistency**: This means how regularly you keep up your outreach efforts. For example, if you reach out to potential clients every day, you're being consistent. So you want to be consistently maximising the outreach rate to the capacity available. I.e. sending 100-200 connection requests week after week (and other methods to increase capacity).

Together, these create your overall Volume:

## ****Outreach Rate x Consistency = Volume****## By focusing on both how many people you reach out to and how often you do it, you can increase your chances of success in finding new clients. See image below as a representation of the difference.

![CleanShot 2025-01-06 at 09.37.42@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2025-01-06_at_09_37_42_2x_e226b393-fb4e-4448-80f6-f756744ee59c.png)

### Understanding Conversion

Conversion is the rate at which your outreach efforts turn into paying clients. For example, if you send out 1,000 connection requests on LinkedIn and gain one client, your conversion rate is 0.1%. This is a good target to aim for. Another coach might need to send 10,000 requests to get one client, resulting in a 0.01% conversion rate. Our goal is to achieve the highest conversion rate possible.****Different Levels of Conversion****1.****Macro Conversion****: This is the big goal—turning a prospect into a paying client. It's the ultimate success metric for your outreach efforts.

2.  ****Milestone Conversions****: These are the key steps along the way to a macro conversion. For example, moving an interested lead to a booked call, then to a discovery call, and finally to a sales call. Each milestone is important for guiding prospects through the sales journey.
    
3.  ****Micro Conversions****: These are the small actions that lead to milestone conversions. For instance, for a prospect to become interested, they need to see and accept your connection request, then reply to express interest. For them to book a call, they need to read your message, click a link, and schedule the call.
    

Understanding these levels helps you see where you can improve each step of the process to boost your overall conversion rate.

#### ***What Influences Conversion?***#### 

####******Conversion is influenced by two main factors: ****Pain**** and*** Confidence***. These are the driving forces that motivate a potential client to take action.

1.  ****Pain****: This represents the problem or need your potential client is experiencing. The greater the discomfort or urgency they feel, the more likely they are to seek a solution.
    
2.  ****Confidence****: This is the trust and belief that your potential client has in your ability to solve their problem. The more confidence they have in you and your offer, the more likely they are to take action.
    

You can think of it like this:

(Pain Level + Confidence Level) / 2 = Conversion (Likelihood of Action)

If a person's pain and confidence levels are high enough to cross their action threshold, they're more likely to become your client. By increasing the perceived pain of staying the same and boosting confidence in your solution, you enhance the chance of conversion.

### What Is This?

This lesson will guide you through the process of choosing the most suitable business structure for your coaching practice, focusing on Sole Trader and Limited Liability Company (LLC or Ltd) options.

### Why Is This Important?

Choosing the right business structure will help you:

-   ****Protect against liabilities:****Understand your personal risk.  
    -****Optimize taxation:****Ensure you are taxed appropriately.  
    -****Manage administrative overhead:****Understand the complexity of running your chosen structure.

Legally we cannot advise on business structures, you need to speak with your accountants. However, the majority of our coaches trade as a limited company in the UK or LLC in the USA.

### Business Structure Comparison****Aspect****

****Sole Trader****

****Limited Liability Company (LLC or Ltd)****

****Definition****A sole trader is an individual running a business on their own.

A separate legal entity from its owners.****Liability****Personal liability for all business debts and obligations.

Personal assets are protected from business liabilities.****Taxation****Simplified tax process; profit is taxed as personal income.

Separate tax entity; potential tax benefits and complexities.****Administrative Overhead****Easier and less costly to set up and run.

More complicated and costly to set up and maintain.

### Steps to Choose Your Business Structure

1.****Evaluate Your Needs:****2. - Assess your need for liability protection.  
- Consider tax implications and benefits.  
- Think about the administrative burden you can handle.

3.****Utilize Government and Professional Resources:****  
4. - Visit government websites for definitions and implications of each structure.  
- Use professional advisory sites to get a comprehensive understanding.

* * *

### Consult Recommended Professionals

****UK:****-****Accountants:****Seek advice on whether to choose Sole Trader or Ltd.  
-****Resources:****HMRC website, professional advisory organizations.****USA****-****Tax Advisors:****Get insights into LLC structuring and implications at state and federal level.  
-****Resources:****IRS website, Small Business Administration (SBA).

### Practical Tips

-****Document Your Findings:****Keep notes on pros and cons relevant to your situation.  
-****Ask Specific Questions:****In consultations, ask about specific scenarios or concerns.  
-****Plan for the Future:**** Consider how your business might grow and how that affects your choice.

### Summary

Choosing the correct business structure is not a big decision and can be changed later if needed. DO NOT let it hold up you making progress through the Academy. By researching your options and consulting with professionals, you can make an informed decision that protects you from liabilities, optimises your taxes, and balances administrative demands.

This sets you up for long-term success and enables you to focus on what you do best: coaching your clients to achieve their goals.
